questions.question: Consider the following formula and its two interpretations I_1 and I_2. alpha : (forall x)[P_x <=> (forall y)[Q_xy <=> ~Q_yy]] => (forall x)[~P_x]. I_1 : Domain = the set of natural numbers; P_x = 'x is a prime number'; Q_xy = 'y divides x'. I_2 : same as I_1 except that P_x = 'x is a composite number'. Which of the following statements is true? A. I_1 satisfies alpha, I_2 does not   B. I_2 satisfies alpha, I_1 does not   C. Neither I_1 nor I_2 satisfies alpha   D. Both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha
questions.answer: D. Both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha
questions.to_find: Which interpretation(s) make the closed formula alpha true

understand.plain: The formula alpha is an implication: (antecedent) => (forall x)[~P_x]. The trick is to evaluate the antecedent first. The antecedent forces P_x to be equivalent to the inner statement (forall y)[Q_xy <=> ~Q_yy]. Since every number divides itself, Q_yy is always true, so ~Q_yy is always false. That collapses the inner statement, and once you see the antecedent is false in both interpretations, the implication is vacuously true regardless of what P means. So both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha.

keywords.explain: An implication A => B is true whenever its antecedent A is false, no matter what B says. You never even need to check B.
keywords.example: 'If 2 is odd then the moon is cheese' is true because the antecedent (2 is odd) is false.

keywords.explain: A <=> B is true exactly when A and B have the same truth value (both true or both false).
keywords.example: Q_xy <=> ~Q_yy is true only when 'y divides x' agrees with 'y does not divide y'.

keywords.explain: In the natural numbers every y divides itself, so Q_yy = 'y divides y' is always true and ~Q_yy is always false.
keywords.example: 5 divides 5, 7 divides 7, so Q_yy holds for every y.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='478' height='248' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Q_yy = 'y divides y' is always TRUE</text><text x='20' y='72' class='lbl ink'>so ~Q_yy is always FALSE</text><text x='20' y='112' class='lbl mut'>antecedent (forall x)[P_x <=> ...] = FALSE</text><line x1='20' y1='128' x2='460' y2='128' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='162' class='lbl ink'>FALSE => anything</text><text x='20' y='196' class='big ok'>alpha is TRUE (vacuously)</text><text x='20' y='228' class='sub mut'>same conclusion for I_1 (prime) and I_2 (composite)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A reasoning panel showing that because y divides y is always true, the antecedent of alpha is false, making the implication vacuously true under both interpretations.

given.aim: Decide whether the closed first-order formula alpha is true under interpretation I_1, under I_2, both, or neither.

terms.example: Its truth value is computed by first evaluating the antecedent
terms.connects: An implication is false only when antecedent is true and conclusion false

terms.meaning: The predicate 'y divides x' over the natural numbers
terms.example: Q_(6)(3) is true (3 divides 6); Q_(6)(4) is false

terms.meaning: The instance 'y divides y', which holds for every natural number y
terms.connects: Makes ~Q_yy uniformly false, collapsing the biconditional

terms.meaning: 'x is prime' in I_1 and 'x is composite' in I_2
terms.example: P_(7) true in I_1; P_(8) true in I_2
terms.connects: Only the antecedent and conclusion mention P_x, but the antecedent is already false

given.plan: Evaluate the inner statement (forall y)[Q_xy <=> ~Q_yy], show the antecedent of alpha is false in both interpretations, then conclude the implication is vacuously true regardless of how P is interpreted.

steps.title: Collapse the inner statement using reflexivity of divisibility
steps.formula_raw: A <=> B is true iff A and B have the same truth value
steps.apply: For any y, Q_yy = 'y divides y' is true, so ~Q_yy is false. The inner statement becomes (forall y)[Q_xy <=> false], i.e. (forall y)[~Q_xy], meaning 'no y divides x'. But y = 1 divides every x, so this is false for every x.
steps.note: The variable y is bound inside, so ~Q_yy is a fixed false value as y ranges; it does not depend on x.

steps.title: Evaluate the antecedent of alpha
steps.apply: Since the inner statement is false for every x, the antecedent (forall x)[P_x <=> false] is the same as (forall x)[~P_x]. In I_1 this asserts 'every natural number is not prime' (false, since 2 is prime); in I_2 it asserts 'every natural number is not composite' (false, since 4 is composite). So the antecedent of alpha is FALSE in both I_1 and I_2.
steps.note: We never need the actual conclusion (forall x)[~P_x] once the antecedent is known to be false.

steps.title: Apply the vacuous-truth rule for implication
steps.formula_raw: A => B is true whenever A is false
steps.apply: alpha has the shape (antecedent) => (conclusion). The antecedent is false under both interpretations, so the implication alpha is true under both. Hence both I_1 and I_2 satisfy alpha.
steps.note: This is exactly the vacuous case: a false premise makes the whole conditional true.

solution.result: Both interpretations make the antecedent false, so alpha is vacuously true under both: answer D.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the vacuous-truth rule A => B is true when A is false; because y divides y is always true, the antecedent of alpha collapses to a false statement, so alpha holds under both I_1 and I_2.

questions.question: Identify the correct translation into logical notation of the following assertion: 'Some boys in the class are taller than all the girls.' Note: taller(x, y) is true if x is taller than y. A. (exists x)(boy(x) -> (forall y)(girl(y) and taller(x, y)))   B. (exists x)(boy(x) and (forall y)(girl(y) and taller(x, y)))   C. (exists x)(boy(x) -> (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x, y)))   D. (exists x)(boy(x) and (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x, y)))
questions.answer: D. (exists x)(boy(x) and (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x, y)))
questions.to_find: The correct first-order translation of the English sentence

understand.plain: The sentence says there is at least one boy who is taller than every girl. 'Some boy' is an existential quantifier paired with 'and' (you assert the witness IS a boy and that he has the property). 'taller than all the girls' is a universal over y, and inside a universal the correct connective is 'implies' (for every y, IF y is a girl THEN our boy is taller than y). Matching these two patterns gives option D.

keywords.explain: 'Some A is/has B' translates to (exists x)(A(x) and B(x)); you conjoin the type with the property.
'All A have B' translates to (forall y)(A(y) -> B(y)); you guard the universal with an implication, not a conjunction.
keywords.example: 'All birds fly' = (forall y)(bird(y) -> fly(y)), not bird(y) and fly(y).

keywords.explain: The natural pairings are exists+and and forall+implies; mixing them (exists+implies or forall+and) usually distorts the meaning.
keywords.example: (exists x)(boy(x) -> ...) is true even if no boys exist, which is wrong here.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='498' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='42' class='lbl ink'>'Some boy'  ->  exists x ( boy(x) AND ... )</text><text x='20' y='86' class='lbl ink'>'taller than all girls'</text><text x='40' y='116' class='lbl ink'>->  forall y ( girl(y) -> taller(x,y) )</text><line x1='20' y1='134' x2='480' y2='134' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='172' class='sub mut'>exists pairs with AND; forall pairs with IMPLIES</text><text x='20' y='208' class='big ok'>Option D</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A pattern-matching panel showing 'some boy' maps to exists-with-and and 'taller than all girls' maps to forall-with-implies, yielding option D.

given.aim: Translate 'Some boys in the class are taller than all the girls' into correct first-order logic.

terms.meaning: Predicate that is true when x is a boy
terms.example: Used as the type guard for the existential witness
terms.connects: Joined by 'and' under (exists x) to assert the witness is actually a boy

terms.meaning: Predicate that is true when y is a girl
terms.example: Used as the antecedent of the inner implication
terms.connects: Guards the universal: only girls y need to be considered

terms.meaning: True when x is taller than y
terms.example: taller(boyA, girlB) means boyA is taller than girlB
terms.connects: The consequent that must hold for every girl y

terms.meaning: An outer (exists x) over boys and an inner (forall y) over girls
terms.example: exists x (boy(x) and forall y (girl(y) -> taller(x,y)))
terms.connects: exists pairs with and, forall pairs with implies

given.plan: Apply the standard pairings: 'some boy' becomes (exists x)(boy(x) and ...) and 'taller than all girls' becomes (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)); then identify which option matches.

steps.title: Translate 'some boy' with existential and conjunction
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property R' = (exists x)(A(x) and R(x))
steps.apply: 'Some boys ... are R' becomes (exists x)(boy(x) and R(x)). This rules out options A and C, which wrongly use boy(x) -> ..., because an implication would be satisfied even when no boys exist.
steps.note: Using -> after exists is the classic trap; the sentence claims an actual tall boy exists, so 'and' is required.

steps.title: Translate 'taller than all the girls' with universal and implication
steps.formula_raw: 'taller than all girls' = (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x, y))
steps.apply: Substituting R(x) gives (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y)): for every y, if y is a girl then x is taller than y. This rules out option B, which uses girl(y) and taller(x,y) and would wrongly require every object y to be a girl.
steps.note: Inside forall you guard with implies; 'and' would falsely assert that everything in the domain is a girl.

steps.title: Assemble the full formula
steps.apply: Combining both parts gives (exists x)(boy(x) and (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x,y))), which is exactly option D.
steps.note: Both correct pairings (exists+and, forall+implies) appear, confirming D.

solution.result: The correct translation is option D: (exists x)(boy(x) and (forall y)(girl(y) -> taller(x, y))).

questions.formula_note: The decisive rules are 'some' = exists + and and 'all' = forall + implies; applying both produces option D and eliminates the implication-after-exists (A, C) and conjunction-after-forall (B) traps.

questions.question: What is the first order predicate calculus statement equivalent to the following? 'Every teacher is liked by some student.' A. (forall x)[teacher(x) -> (exists y)(student(y) -> likes(y, x))]   B. (forall x)[teacher(x) -> (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x))]   C. (exists y)(forall x)[teacher(x) -> (student(y) and likes(y, x))]   D. (forall x)[teacher(x) and (exists y)(student(y) -> likes(y, x))]
questions.answer: B. (forall x)[teacher(x) -> (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x))]
questions.to_find: The correct first-order predicate-calculus translation of the sentence

understand.plain: 'Every teacher is liked by some student' has two layers. The outer 'every teacher' is a universal guarded by implication: for all x, IF x is a teacher THEN something holds. The inner 'some student likes x' is an existential paired with 'and': there exists a y that IS a student AND likes x. Putting the universal-implies outside and existential-and inside gives option B.

keywords.explain: 'Every A ...' becomes (forall x)(A(x) -> ...); the implication restricts attention to objects that are A.
'Some A does B' becomes (exists y)(A(y) and B(y)); you conjoin being A with doing B.
keywords.example: 'Some student likes x' = (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x)).

keywords.explain: Because the student can differ from teacher to teacher, the existential must sit INSIDE the universal, not be pulled out front.
keywords.example: Option C wrongly puts (exists y) outside, forcing one single student to like every teacher.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 510 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='508' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='42' class='lbl ink'>'Every teacher'  ->  forall x ( teacher(x) -> ... )</text><text x='20' y='86' class='lbl ink'>'is liked by some student'</text><text x='40' y='116' class='lbl ink'>->  exists y ( student(y) AND likes(y,x) )</text><line x1='20' y1='134' x2='490' y2='134' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='172' class='sub mut'>forall pairs with IMPLIES, exists pairs with AND, exists stays inside</text><text x='20' y='208' class='big ok'>Option B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A pattern panel showing 'every teacher' maps to forall-with-implies and 'liked by some student' maps to a nested exists-with-and, giving option B.

given.aim: Translate 'Every teacher is liked by some student' into correct first-order predicate calculus.

terms.meaning: Predicate true when x is a teacher
terms.example: Guards the outer universal via teacher(x) -> ...

terms.meaning: Predicate true when y is a student
terms.example: Conjoined with likes(y, x) under the inner existential
terms.connects: Joined by 'and' to assert the liker is actually a student

terms.example: likes(studentA, teacherB) means studentA likes teacherB
terms.connects: The property the existential student must have toward the teacher x

terms.meaning: Outer (forall x) over teachers, inner (exists y) over students
terms.example: forall x (teacher(x) -> exists y (student(y) and likes(y,x)))
terms.connects: Keeping exists inside lets the liking student depend on the teacher

given.plan: Apply 'every' = forall + implies for the outer layer and 'some' = exists + and for the inner layer, keeping the existential nested inside the universal, then match to the options.

steps.title: Translate 'every teacher ...' with universal and implication
steps.apply: 'Every teacher is ...' becomes (forall x)[teacher(x) -> ...]. This eliminates option D, which uses teacher(x) and ..., wrongly asserting that every object in the domain is a teacher.
steps.note: After forall you guard with implies; 'and' would over-claim that everything is a teacher.

steps.title: Translate 'liked by some student' with existential and conjunction
steps.formula_raw: 'some student likes x' = (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x))
steps.apply: The inner clause becomes (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x)). This eliminates option A, which uses student(y) -> likes(y,x); that implication is satisfied even by a non-student y, so it fails to assert a real liking student exists.
steps.note: After exists you conjoin with 'and'; -> here is the classic trap that trivially holds for non-students.

steps.title: Keep the existential nested and assemble
steps.apply: The existential must stay inside the universal so the liking student may differ per teacher. Option C pulls (exists y) to the front, forcing one student to like all teachers, which is stronger than the sentence. The correct nesting gives option B.
steps.note: Pulling exists outside changes the meaning from 'each teacher has some liker' to 'one liker for everyone'.

solution.result: The correct statement is option B: (forall x)[teacher(x) -> (exists y)(student(y) and likes(y, x))].

questions.formula_note: Combining 'every' = forall + implies (outer) with 'some' = exists + and (inner, nested) yields option B; the traps are conjunction after forall (D), implication after exists (A), and pulling the existential outside (C).

formulas.name: Truth Table of Implication (and Vacuous Truth)
formulas.plain: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
formulas.whenToUse: Use to evaluate or simplify any conditional, especially to recognise that a false antecedent makes the whole implication vacuously true without checking the conclusion.
formulas.trap: A false antecedent makes A => B true regardless of B (vacuous truth); do not assume the conclusion must hold just because the implication is true.

formulas.name: Truth Rule of the Biconditional
formulas.plain: A <=> B is true exactly when A and B have the same truth value
formulas.whenToUse: Use to evaluate a biconditional; in particular when one side is a fixed truth value, A <=> false reduces to ~A and A <=> true reduces to A.
formulas.trap: A <=> false is NOT always false; it equals ~A, so it is true when A is false. Always pin down the constant side before judging.

formulas.name: Translating 'Some' with Existential and Conjunction
formulas.plain: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
formulas.whenToUse: Use when translating English statements of the form 'some/there exists an A that is B' into first-order logic; the existential quantifier pairs with a conjunction.

terms.means: there exists at least one object x
the type or category predicate (e.g. boy, student)
the property asserted of that object

formulas.trap: Do not write (exists x)(A(x) -> B(x)); an implication is satisfied by any object that is not an A, so it fails to assert that a genuine A with property B exists.

formulas.name: Translating 'All' with Universal and Implication
formulas.plain: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))
formulas.whenToUse: Use when translating English statements of the form 'every/all A is/are B' into first-order logic; the universal quantifier pairs with an implication that guards the type.

terms.means: for every object x in the domain
the type or category predicate that restricts the universal
the property claimed for all such objects

formulas.trap: Do not write (forall x)(A(x) and B(x)); a conjunction wrongly asserts that every object in the entire domain is an A, instead of restricting to A's.
